macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Ten or Eleven Levels Between Kodesh and Terumah

micro_segments.title: Overview of mishna listing distinctions between Kodesh and Terumah
Focus on stratifications and treatment of different categories of food

macro_segments.title: Vessels in Vessels (Kelim b'Kelim) and the Two Explanations for the Kodesh Restriction

micro_segments.title: Basic principle: vessels can be placed within vessels for Terumah but not Kodesh in mikvah immersion
Rabbi Ila's explanation: weight of inner vessel prevents water from passing between them (chatzitza concern)
Gemara challenge: why conflate two distinct chatzitza concerns (vessels and knotted garments)?
Detailed explanation of both chatzitza concerns and their distinct nature
Rabbi Ila's position: ten levels not eleven, conflating vessel and garment cases
Enumeration and categorization of all eleven distinctions from the mishna

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina bar Pappa's Division: First Five (or Six) and Last Five Stringencies

micro_segments.title: First five stringencies apply to both actual Kodesh and chulin treated with Kodesh sanctity
Last five stringencies apply only to actual Kodesh, not to chulin treated as Kodesh
Gemara explanation: first five connected to biblical tumah concerns
Last five not connected to biblical tumah, only rabbinic stringencies for actual Kodesh
Extended analysis of which distinctions fall into which category and the logic behind it

macro_segments.title: Rava's Alternative Explanation: Narrow-Mouthed Vessels and Needles Concern

micro_segments.title: Rava's position: vessels and knotted garments are not the same chatzitza concern
Rava's reasoning: concern about immersing small items like needles in narrow-mouthed vessels
Explanation of shfoferet hanod (tube width) as required opening size for water connection
Modern mikvah application: combining rain water reservoir with immersion pool via proper-sized pipe

macro_segments.title: Practical Difference Between Rabbi Ila and Rava: Application to Baskets

micro_segments.title: Question: does the stringency apply to baskets and woven vessels with wide openings?
According to Rabbi Ila's chatzitza reasoning, baskets remain problematic like all vessels
According to Rava's narrow-opening reasoning, baskets are categorically excluded

macro_segments.title: Vessels Within Vessels: Conditions for Purity Transfer Without Direct Mikvah Contact

micro_segments.title: Extended discussion of porous vessels, baskets, and whether they truly divide a mikvah
Gemara reinterprets mishna case: outer vessel's purity status affects inner vessels
If outer vessel was tamei and becomes tahor through mikvah, inner vessels become tahor automatically
If outer vessel was already tahor, inner vessels only become tahor if opening is shfoferet hanod size
Mishna case explained: filling vessels with vessels and immersing them results in purity

macro_segments.title: Practical Difference Between Rabbi Ila and Rava: Application to Baskets (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tannaitic dispute mirrors this disagreement: Abba Shaul vs. anonymous first Tanna on basket immersion

macro_segments.title: Concern for Common People (Am Ha'aretz) Following Example in Kodesh versus Terumah

micro_segments.title: Gemara question: if stringencies apply to Terumah, why not apply to Kodesh since knowledgeable people will be careful?
Answer: concerned that am ha'aretz will imitate improper practices for Kodesh despite seeing proper example
Counter-objection: same concern should apply to Terumah; answer is that we don't accept Terumah from am ha'aretz
Distinction explained: Terumah can be given to another Kohen; Kodesh korbanot have no alternative destination
Therefore stricter standards for Kodesh are necessary to set proper example for am ha'aretz

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yossi on Accepting Wine and Oil at the Beit Hamikdash: Preventing Schism

micro_segments.title: Braita: all people are trusted regarding purity of wine and oil brought to Beit Hamikdash year-round
Reason: if rejected, they will build their own private altar rather than participate in Temple service
Principle of not causing hatred (eiva) overrides normal witness credibility requirements
Modern application: accepting testimony from am ha'aretz follows Rabbi Yossi's approach to prevent rejection
Broader theme: concern for unity and inclusion sometimes overrides stringent halachic requirements

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Ila vs. Rava (vessel weight vs. narrow opening reason)
Mikvah immersion standards for Kodesh vs. Terumah
Chulin she'na'asu al taharat Kodesh (ordinary food treated with Kodesh sanctity)
Ten or Eleven levels of distinction
Rabbi Chanina bar Pappa division (first five, last five)
Am Ha'aretz following example and eiva (causing hatred)
Beit Hamikdash and prevention of schism
Wine and oil (yayin v'shemen) acceptance standards
Tumah de'oraita (biblical tumah) vs. de'rabanan (rabbinic tumah)
